---
title: "Ch 10b — Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)"
subtitle: "Two foci instead of one — and a constant sum or difference of distances"
lesson-id: "cbse11.conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 10
ncert-chapter-title: "Conic Sections"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.conic-sections-circle-parabola"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.three-d-geometry"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state the standard equation of a circle?
2. Are you fluent with completing the square?
3. Have you understood the focus-directrix definition of a parabola?
4. Can you compute $\sqrt{a^2 - b^2}$ for given $a, b$?

Three confident yeses — read on. Otherwise revisit
**[Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](../10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)**.
:::

## Touch

Pin two thumbtacks into a board, $4$ cm apart. Take a string $10$ cm
long and tie its ends to the two tacks. Pull the string taut with a
pencil and trace a curve while keeping the string taut.

You have just drawn an [**ellipse**](/glossary.qmd#ellipse): every point
on the curve has the property that the *sum* of its distances to the two
tacks equals $10$ (the string length).

If instead you used the *difference* of distances staying constant —
that traces a [**hyperbola**](/glossary.qmd#hyperbola) (the gardener
trick is harder; we use the equation directly).

The widget below sweeps the semi-axes of the ellipse $x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1$.

```{ojs}
viewof aE = Inputs.range([1, 6], {value: 4, step: 0.1, label: "a (semi-major axis)"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof bE = Inputs.range([0.5, 6], {value: 3, step: 0.1, label: "b (semi-minor axis)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const a = aE, b = bE;
  if (b > a) return html`<div style="color:#a00;">Need b ≤ a for the standard horizontal ellipse.</div>`;
  const c = Math.sqrt(a * a - b * b);
  const e = c / a;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Ellipse:</b> x²/a² + y²/b² = 1</div>
    <div>a = ${a.toFixed(2)}, b = ${b.toFixed(2)}, c = √(a² − b²) = ${c.toFixed(3)}</div>
    <div><b>Foci:</b> (${(-c).toFixed(2)}, 0) and (${c.toFixed(2)}, 0)</div>
    <div><b>Eccentricity</b> e = c/a = ${e.toFixed(3)} (between 0 and 1)</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      Sum of distances from any point on the ellipse to the two foci = 2a.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

When $b = a$, the ellipse degenerates to a circle. As $b/a \to 0$, the
ellipse stretches into something flat. The ratio $e = c/a$ measures
how "squashed" it is.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. The Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse. Where does the Sun sit — at
   the centre or at a focus?
2. Suppose you stretch an ellipse with semi-axes $a, b$ until $a = b$.
   What does it become? What if $a > b$? $b > a$?
3. The hyperbola $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ has two
   branches. Why two, not one?
4. As $a \to \infty$ in the ellipse equation, the curve flattens to a
   pair of horizontal lines. What does that suggest the hyperbola is
   "doing"?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) hints at the **eccentricity** $e$ as the master parameter:
$e = 0$ circle, $0 < e < 1$ ellipse, $e = 1$ parabola, $e > 1$
hyperbola. (4) is the **asymptote** picture — the hyperbola lives
between two slanted lines.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Standard ellipse

Sketch $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{16} = 1$. Find the foci.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a^2 = 25, b^2 = 16$. $c^2 = a^2 - b^2 = 9$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 5, b = 4, c = 3$. Foci $(\pm 3, 0)$. Vertices $(\pm 5, 0)$ and
co-vertices $(0, \pm 4)$. Eccentricity $e = 3/5$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Eccentricity and shape

Two ellipses: $E_1$ has $e = 0.1$, $E_2$ has $e = 0.9$. Which is closer
to a circle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$e = 0$ is a circle. Smaller $e$ means rounder.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$E_1$ (with $e = 0.1$) is nearly circular. $E_2$ is highly elongated.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Standard hyperbola

Sketch $\dfrac{x^2}{16} - \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$. Find the foci and
asymptotes.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a^2 = 16, b^2 = 9$. For a hyperbola, $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 4, b = 3, c = 5$. Foci $(\pm 5, 0)$.

Asymptotes: $y = \pm \dfrac{b}{a} x = \pm \dfrac{3}{4} x$.

Vertices $(\pm 4, 0)$. Eccentricity $e = c/a = 5/4 > 1$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Find equation given foci

Find the equation of the ellipse with foci $(\pm 4, 0)$ and vertices
$(\pm 5, 0)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 5, c = 4 \Rightarrow b^2 = a^2 - c^2 = 9$.

Equation: $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Hyperbola from foci

Find the equation of the hyperbola with foci $(\pm 5, 0)$ and vertices
$(\pm 3, 0)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 3, c = 5 \Rightarrow b^2 = c^2 - a^2 = 16$.

$\dfrac{x^2}{9} - \dfrac{y^2}{16} = 1$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Latus rectum

Find the length of the latus rectum of $\dfrac{x^2}{36} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For an ellipse, latus rectum length $= 2b^2/a$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a^2 = 36 \Rightarrow a = 6$. $b^2 = 9$. Latus rectum $= 2 \cdot 9 / 6 = 3$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Vertical major axis

Sketch $\dfrac{x^2}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{25} = 1$. Identify the major axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The denominator under $y^2$ ($25$) is larger, so the major axis is
vertical.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Major axis vertical, semi-major $5$ along $y$, semi-minor $4$ along $x$.

$c^2 = 25 - 16 = 9 \Rightarrow c = 3$. Foci $(0, \pm 3)$.
</details>

You have just used the **standard ellipse** and **standard hyperbola**
equations, **eccentricity**, **latus rectum**, **foci-from-equation**,
and the **asymptote** picture for hyperbolas. Now we name them.

::: {.fun-fact}
**Kepler discovers the elliptical orbit (1609).** Tycho Brahe spent
decades recording the positions of Mars to a fraction of an arc-minute.
Johannes Kepler inherited the data, tried over forty circle-and-epicycle
combinations, then tried a single ellipse with the Sun at one focus —
and the residuals collapsed to noise. *Astronomia Nova* announced what is
now Kepler's First Law: planets sweep ellipses, not circles. Halley's
comet, which you have probably already heard of, follows a *highly*
elongated ellipse with $e \approx 0.967$ — close enough to a parabola
that early astronomers argued whether it was bound at all.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**One parameter, four shapes.** Eccentricity $e$ is the master dial:
$e = 0$ circle, $0 < e < 1$ ellipse, $e = 1$ parabola, $e > 1$
hyperbola. For ellipses $c^2 = a^2 - b^2$; for hyperbolas $c^2 = a^2
+ b^2$ — the sign flips because the foci of a hyperbola sit *outside*
its vertices, while the foci of an ellipse sit *inside*. Memorise the
sign convention and the rest of the chapter is bookkeeping.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Ellipse.** Locus of points whose *sum* of distances to two fixed
points (foci) is constant.

Standard form (horizontal major axis):
$$ \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1, \quad a > b > 0. $$

- Foci: $(\pm c, 0)$ with $c = \sqrt{a^2 - b^2}$.
- Vertices: $(\pm a, 0)$. Co-vertices: $(0, \pm b)$.
- **Eccentricity:** $e = c/a$, with $0 \le e < 1$.
- **Latus rectum:** $\dfrac{2 b^2}{a}$.

If $b > a$: major axis is vertical; the formulas swap roles.

**Hyperbola.** Locus of points whose *difference* of distances to two
fixed points is constant.

Standard form (horizontal transverse axis):
$$ \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1. $$

- Foci: $(\pm c, 0)$ with $c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.
- Vertices: $(\pm a, 0)$.
- **Asymptotes:** $y = \pm \dfrac{b}{a} x$.
- **Eccentricity:** $e = c/a > 1$.
- **Latus rectum:** $\dfrac{2 b^2}{a}$.

**Eccentricity dictionary:**

| Curve | $e$ |
|:--|:--|
| Circle | $0$ |
| Ellipse | $0 < e < 1$ |
| Parabola | $e = 1$ |
| Hyperbola | $e > 1$ |

:::

A short verse:

> *Ellipse: sum of distances stays the same.*
> *Hyperbola: difference is the cleaner game.*
> *Larger denominator rules the longer axis;*
> *Asymptote slope is $b$ over $a$.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the equation of the ellipse whose major axis is along the
> $x$-axis, whose semi-major axis is $5$, and which passes through the
> point $(4, 12/5)$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Major axis along $x$-axis: $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$.
2. Substitute $(4, 12/5)$: $\dfrac{16}{25} + \dfrac{144/25}{b^2} = 1
   \Rightarrow \dfrac{144/25}{b^2} = \dfrac{9}{25} \Rightarrow b^2 = 16$.
3. Equation: $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{16} = 1$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Foci of $\dfrac{x^2}{49} + \dfrac{y^2}{36} = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c^2 = 49 - 36 = 13$. Foci $(\pm \sqrt{13}, 0)$.
</details>

**W2.** Eccentricity of $\dfrac{x^2}{16} - \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5$, $a = 4$. $e = 5/4$.
</details>

**W3.** Asymptotes of $\dfrac{x^2}{4} - \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = \pm \dfrac{3}{2} x$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find the equation of the ellipse whose foci are $(\pm 2, 0)$
and eccentricity is $1/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$c = 2$, $e = c/a = 1/2 \Rightarrow a = 4$, $b^2 = a^2 - c^2 = 12$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x^2}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{12} = 1$.
</details>

**P2.** Find the eccentricity of the ellipse whose latus rectum is half
its major axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\dfrac{2 b^2}{a} = \dfrac{2a}{2} = a$, so $b^2 = a^2/2$.
Use $b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a^2/2 = a^2(1 - e^2) \Rightarrow 1 - e^2 = 1/2 \Rightarrow e^2 = 1/2
\Rightarrow e = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
</details>

**P3.** Find the equation of the hyperbola with vertices $(\pm 7, 0)$
and eccentricity $4/3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a = 7$, $c = ae = 28/3$, $b^2 = c^2 - a^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c^2 = 784/9$, $b^2 = 784/9 - 49 = 343/9$.

$\dfrac{x^2}{49} - \dfrac{9 y^2}{343} = 1$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that the line $y = mx + c$ is tangent to the ellipse
$\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ if and only if
$c^2 = a^2 m^2 + b^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute the line into the ellipse, get a quadratic in $x$. The
discriminant is zero for tangency.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{(mx + c)^2}{b^2} = 1
\Rightarrow b^2 x^2 + a^2 (mx + c)^2 = a^2 b^2
\Rightarrow (b^2 + a^2 m^2) x^2 + 2 a^2 m c x + a^2(c^2 - b^2) = 0$.

Discriminant zero: $4 a^4 m^2 c^2 - 4(b^2 + a^2 m^2)(a^2)(c^2 - b^2) = 0$,

i.e. $a^2 m^2 c^2 = (b^2 + a^2 m^2)(c^2 - b^2)$.

Expanding gives $c^2 = b^2 + a^2 m^2$, as required. ✓
</details>

**S2.** Reflection property of an ellipse: a ray from one focus, after
striking the ellipse, passes through the other focus. Use the
sum-of-distances definition to argue why.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At any point $P$, the sum $|PF_1| + |PF_2|$ is locally constant. The
unit vectors from $P$ to $F_1$ and to $F_2$ thus make equal angles with
the tangent.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Differentiating $|PF_1| + |PF_2| = 2a$ along the curve gives that the
projections of the unit vectors $\hat{u}_1 = (P - F_1)/|PF_1|$ and
$\hat{u}_2 = (P - F_2)/|PF_2|$ onto the tangent direction are equal in
magnitude but opposite in sign. Equivalently, the angle between
$\hat{u}_1$ and the tangent equals the angle between the tangent and
$\hat{u}_2$ — exactly the law of reflection.

This is why **whispering galleries** (e.g. St Paul's Cathedral) work:
a whisper from one focus is heard at the other.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The eccentricity of the ellipse
$\dfrac{x^2}{36} + \dfrac{y^2}{20} = 1$ is
(a) $1/3$ (b) $2/3$ (c) $4/3$ (d) $\sqrt{5}/3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c = \sqrt{36 - 20} = 4$, $a = 6$. $e = 4/6 = 2/3$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the foci and length of the latus rectum
of $25 x^2 + 4 y^2 = 100$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide by $100$ to get standard form.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x^2}{4} + \dfrac{y^2}{25} = 1$. Major along $y$: $a^2 = 25, b^2 = 4$.

$c^2 = 21 \Rightarrow c = \sqrt{21}$. Foci $(0, \pm\sqrt{21})$.

Latus rectum $= 2 b^2/a = 8/5$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Find the equation of the hyperbola with
foci $(0, \pm 5)$ and vertices $(0, \pm 3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Vertical transverse axis. $a = 3, c = 5 \Rightarrow b^2 = 16$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{y^2}{9} - \dfrac{x^2}{16} = 1$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** An arch is in the shape of half an ellipse,
$8$ m wide at the base and $3$ m high at the centre. Take the origin at
the centre of the base and the $x$-axis along the ground.

(i) Find the equation of the ellipse.
(ii) Find the height of the arch at a point $1.5$ m from the centre.
(iii) Find the foci of the full ellipse.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Half-width $= a = 4$. Half-height $= b = 3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\dfrac{x^2}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

**(ii)** At $x = 1.5$: $\dfrac{2.25}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1
\Rightarrow \dfrac{y^2}{9} = \dfrac{13.75}{16} \Rightarrow y^2 = \dfrac{123.75}{16}
\Rightarrow y \approx 2.78$ m. (Take positive root.)

**(iii)** $c^2 = 16 - 9 = 7 \Rightarrow c = \sqrt{7}$. Foci $(\pm\sqrt{7}, 0)$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 10 LA "elliptical arch / bridge"
matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the tangent-line condition (Stretch S1) is JEE Main
standard.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$c^2 = a^2 - b^2$ for both ellipse and hyperbola."** No. Ellipse:
   $c^2 = a^2 - b^2$. Hyperbola: $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$. The sign flips
   because the hyperbola's foci are *outside* the vertices.
2. **"The hyperbola has only one branch."** Two — one for each sign of
   the difference $|PF_1| - |PF_2|$.
3. **"Eccentricity tells you the size."** No — eccentricity tells you
   the *shape*. Two ellipses can have the same $e$ but very different
   sizes.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Eccentricity is the shape dial.** $e = 0$ circle, $0 < e < 1$
   ellipse, $e = 1$ parabola, $e > 1$ hyperbola — one number tells you
   which curve you are looking at.
2. **Larger denominator wins the major axis.** Look at
   $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$: whichever denominator is
   larger names the long direction (and the foci).
3. **Sign of $b^2$ flips between conics.** Ellipse: $c^2 = a^2 - b^2$.
   Hyperbola: $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$. The flip is the *whole* algebraic
   difference between the two.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state the eccentricity dictionary for circle / ellipse /
   parabola / hyperbola from memory?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you spot the major axis direction (horizontal vs vertical) by
   looking at which denominator is larger?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Three-Dimensional Geometry Intro](../11-three-d-geometry-intro/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover*. Ellipse and hyperbola structure pays
  back across Class 12 calculus and JEE.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](../10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)** —
  the focus-directrix framework.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Three-Dimensional Geometry](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/11-three-d-geometry-vectors/index.qmd)** —
  conic-section reasoning extends to quadric surfaces (ellipsoids,
  hyperboloids).

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 10.4–10.5: ellipse and hyperbola — definitions, standard
  equations, properties.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t3.conic-sections`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/conic-sections/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Astronomy.** Kepler's First Law: planets move in ellipses
with the Sun at one focus. The reflection property of the ellipse
explains why parabolic dishes (Lesson 10a) focus parallel rays — the
ellipse's two-foci version, with one focus "at infinity", *is* the
parabola.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Hyperbolic trajectories arise in slingshot
manoeuvres for spacecraft and in some power-up paths for racing games.
Asymptotes give a clean upper bound on travel direction.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: Pestalozzi (the gardener's-string ellipse construction),
3Blue1Brown (eccentricity as the master parameter for the conic family),
Russian school (Discover sequence forces the eccentricity dictionary),
Bhāskara (verse on sum vs difference of distances).
:::
